title: From evidence lines to a calibrated claim

objectives.statement: Distinguish what molecular, organismal, population, and intervention evidence can contribute to a causal question and state a material limit of each line.
Weigh evidence lines for reliability, relevance, consistency, and independence rather than treating studies as equal votes.
Integrate a multiscale evidence bundle into a claim that names its strength, causal scope, pivotal support, and remaining uncertainty.
Revise an integrated claim when evidence conflicts and propose a study that discriminates between plausible explanations for the conflict.

scenes.title: One result says yes
scenes.contentHtml:
One result says yes

A research team studies a fictional marsh grass that survives salty soil. In a molecular assay, a protein called Tide-1 attaches to DNA beside a salt-response gene. The result is clean and repeatable. Does Tide-1 cause the whole plant to tolerate salt?

Not yet. The assay has revealed a possible step in a mechanism, but the headline claim crosses several gaps. Does that attachment change the gene? Does the change matter in a whole plant? Does it matter across natural populations? What happens when Tide-1 itself is deliberately changed?

This lesson is about crossing those gaps without pretending they have disappeared. You will combine molecular, whole-organism, population, and intervention evidence into one claim whose strength and limits match the evidence.

The central move is simple to say and demanding to do:

Do not count studies. Ask what each line can show, what could distort it, whether the lines are genuinely independent, and exactly how far their agreement reaches.

The goal is not a universal evidence hierarchy. It is a defensible answer to a bounded question.

Check the two prerequisites

Answer before reading the routing feedback.

  1. A survey finds that plants carrying Tide-1 variant A are more likely to survive in salty estuaries. Name one reason the association might not be causal.
  2. A cell model omits the plant's roots, vascular system, and soil microbes. What must an investigator say before using that model to explain survival of a whole plant?

If you answered that another variable could influence both variant frequency and survival, you used the association–causation distinction needed here. If you answered that the model's assumptions, scale, and missing processes limit the inference, you used the model-limits reasoning needed here. Your wording need not match exactly.

Recovery route

If either prerequisite is unavailable, take this ten-minute bridge before continuing.

  • For association and causation, draw Tide-1 variant → survival. Add a third factor—such as estuary ancestry or local salinity—that could affect both. Until the design handles that rival path, use associated with, not causes.
  • For model limits, complete four prompts: “This model represents ___; it leaves out ___; it is useful for predicting ___; it would fail if ___.”
  • Then retry the two questions above. Continue when you can name one rival explanation for an association and one limit on carrying a model across scales.

You will not be asked to relearn either prerequisite in full. You will use both to decide how much weight each new evidence line deserves.

Callout
label Educational boundary

Every Tide-1 organism, experiment, and result in this lesson is fictional teaching material. No task asks for personal health information, diagnosis, treatment, ingestion, field collection, or unsupervised laboratory work.

Source Note
claims claim-lines-answer-different-questions, claim-weigh-before-integrate, claim-independent-convergence, claim-randomization-boundarysources source-efsa-weight-evidence, source-lawlor-triangulation, source-fda-ich-e9, source-iarc-preamble

The sources support evaluating distinct evidence streams, weighing rather than counting them, checking whether methods have different sources of bias, and treating randomization as protection against bias rather than a guarantee. The Tide-1 case and prerequisite bridge are original fictional teaching material.

scenes.title: What each evidence line can see
scenes.contentHtml:
What each evidence line can see

Imagine looking at the same causal question through four windows. One window shows molecular contact. Another shows a whole organism responding. A third shows patterns across a population. A fourth shows what happens after an investigator deliberately changes something. The views can support one another, but they are not interchangeable.

The first three labels mainly describe where the evidence is observed. “Intervention” describes how a comparison was created. A randomized whole-plant experiment is therefore both organismal and intervention evidence. Treating the four labels as mutually exclusive boxes would hide information about the design.

Four contributions, four boundaries

Evidence line Question it can help answer Main contribution Boundary to inspect
Molecular Do specified molecules interact or change in the proposed sequence? Tests a link in a mechanism; assays may measure binding, structure, reaction, or molecular change. An isolated molecule or cell system may omit metabolism, feedback, tissue context, dose, or time needed in an organism.
Organismal Does the proposed process accompany or alter a phenotype in an intact organism? Tests a phenotype in its intact biological context and, when paired with an assigned change, can support a bounded effect at that scale. Species, strain, life stage, exposure route, dose, and laboratory conditions may not represent the target population.
Population Does exposure or variation track the outcome across people or other organisms, places, or times? Tests reach, heterogeneity, frequency, and association in the target setting. Confounding, selection, measurement error, reverse timing, and population structure can distort an observational pattern.
Intervention Does an assigned change produce a different outcome under the studied conditions? A well-executed randomized comparison can support a bounded causal effect of the assigned intervention. Concealment, blinding, adherence, attrition, outcome measurement, off-target effects, and limited settings can weaken interpretation or generalization.

None of these lines automatically answers, “What is the complete mechanism in every population?” A molecular result may be precise but too narrow. A population association may be broad but causally ambiguous. An intervention may identify an effect without identifying the molecular path that produced it.

The diagram's job is to show sequence without turning the evidence lines into a ladder. You can follow the same sequence without the picture: begin with one question; examine each line; audit quality, fit, bias, and dependence; compare agreement and disagreement; then write the claim.

Weigh before you integrate

For every line, ask three questions.

  1. Reliability: If the study claims to have measured or changed something, how credible is that information? Inspect design, measurement, bias control, missing data, and reporting. This is reliability in the broad evidence-appraisal sense — the credibility of a whole line of evidence — not the narrower measurement sense from Measurement, reliability, and validity (PREM-SCI-006), where reliability meant a measurement's consistency across repeated readings. A perfectly repeatable measurement, reliable in that Lesson 6 sense, can still sit inside a line of evidence you judge not credible here.
  2. Relevance: If the result is reliable, how directly does it answer this question? Match the exposure, outcome, system, population, timing, and dose.
  3. Consistency: Does the result fit the other relevant results? Agreement helps only after checking whether the studies address the same question and whether a shared flaw could produce the same answer.

Then add a fourth question that prevents false convergence: How independent are the important biases? Four analyses of one cohort share its recruitment and measurement errors. Four molecular assays that use the same contaminated reagent can repeat the same artifact. Agreement becomes more informative when different approaches would not be expected to fail in the same way.

Build the causal bridge

Do not assume that evidence must arrive in this order. Use the sequence below as a gap-finding device:

  1. A molecular interaction is possible.
  2. The interaction changes a relevant process.
  3. The process affects an organismal outcome.
  4. The outcome pattern appears in a target population.
  5. Deliberately changing the proposed cause changes the outcome under stated conditions.

Mark each link as supported, contradicted, or still open. A missing link does not erase all other evidence. It limits the claim. For example, consistent molecular and whole-organism experiments might support a mechanism in a model species while leaving effects in natural populations unresolved.

Write only the claim you have earned

A calibrated claim contains five parts:

Direction and relation: what X does or is associated with. Strength: how strongly the integrated evidence supports that relation. Scope: the systems, populations, doses, and times covered. Pivotal support: which evidence lines carry the conclusion. Uncertainty and next test: what remains open and what result would change the conclusion.

Useful strength words include limited, moderate, and strong, but they are not universal numerical grades. The reasons beside the word do the scientific work.

Before moving on, close the table and answer from memory: Why can an intervention be organismal evidence too? Why are four agreeing analyses of one dataset not necessarily four independent confirmations?

Source Note
claims claim-lines-answer-different-questions, claim-weigh-before-integrate, claim-independent-convergence, claim-randomization-boundary, claim-mechanistic-limits, claim-calibrated-conclusionsources source-efsa-weight-evidence, source-lawlor-triangulation, source-fda-ich-e9, source-iarc-preamble

EFSA supports assembling, weighing, and integrating evidence with explicit attention to reliability, relevance, consistency, and uncertainty. Lawlor and colleagues support comparing approaches with different key biases. FDA/ICH E9 supports the bounded role of randomization and blinding. IARC provides an authoritative example of separately evaluating human, experimental-animal, and mechanistic streams before integration, including limits of experimental systems. The four-window explanation, table, bridge, and claim template are original synthesis.

scenes.title: A complete evidence integration
scenes.contentHtml:
A complete evidence integration

This worked example uses invented data. Its purpose is to expose the decisions between reading four study summaries and writing one defensible claim.

Worked Example
id worked-example-tide-onelabel Does Tide-1 contribute to salt tolerance?

Task. Integrate the evidence below to answer: “Does Tide-1 contribute to survival of fictional Seamarsh grass during a 14-day salt exposure?” State the strength and scope of support, the pivotal evidence, the largest uncertainty, and the next study that would most reduce it.

Givens.

Line Design and result Important limitation
Molecular Purified Tide-1 attached to a regulatory DNA sequence beside an ion-transport gene. Replacing six bases in that sequence removed detectable attachment in the same assay. A purified system does not show that attachment changes gene activity or plant survival.
Organismal In one grass background, two independently edited Tide-1 loss-of-function lines and unedited sibling controls were exposed to the same salt regimen. At day 14, 24 of 50 plants from edited line E1 and 25 of 50 from edited line E2 survived; pooled edited survival was therefore 49 of 100, compared with 82 of 100 unedited sibling controls. Both edited lines came from one genetic background; unintended editing effects were checked at predicted sites but cannot be excluded everywhere. The summary does not preserve batch-level allocation or dependence information.
Population Across 800 plants in 12 estuaries, a Tide-1 variant was associated with higher survival after measured salinity was included in the analysis. Estuary ancestry and several habitat differences were not adequately separated from the variant.
Intervention Within each of three grass backgrounds, 100 matched seedlings per arm were randomly assigned to a Tide-1 inhibitor or vehicle. Outcome assessors were masked. Among seedlings with observed outcomes, 50 of 96 (52.1%) survived with inhibitor and 72 of 97 (74.2%) with vehicle. Four inhibitor-assigned and three vehicle-assigned seedlings lacked outcomes after labels detached during masked handling; the reported percentages exclude them. The missing-outcome reason was operational, but whether missingness depended on survival cannot be established. The inhibitor may affect another protein, and the three backgrounds do not represent every estuary population.

The studies are fictional. Counts are supplied for reasoning practice, not to imitate a real species or establish a biological fact.

Unknown. We are not estimating a universal effect size. We need the strongest causal statement that all four lines can jointly support.

Why this model fits. The task asks for evidence integration, so use four operations: check that every line addresses the same bounded question; weigh reliability and relevance; inspect agreement and shared bias; then calibrate the claim.

Plan.

  1. Translate each result into the narrowest claim it supports.
  2. Mark the main threat and the direction it could push the result, when knowable.
  3. Identify which agreements come from genuinely different designs.
  4. Find the weakest bridge from molecule to survival.
  5. Write the claim and try to falsify it with the strongest rival explanation.

Step 1: translate before combining.

  • The molecular assay supports “Tide-1 can attach to this DNA sequence under the assay conditions.” It does not yet support “Tide-1 increases survival.”
  • The organismal comparison supports a Tide-1-related survival difference in one background. Each edited line has lower survival than the pooled controls: 24/50=48%24/50 = 48\% for E1, 25/50=50%25/50 = 50\% for E2, and 82/100=82%82/100 = 82\% for controls. The pooled difference is 49%−82%=−3349\% - 82\% = -33 percentage points for edited versus control plants. Because genotype was deliberately edited, this is also intervention evidence, although the table places it under organismal evidence to emphasize scale.
  • The population study supports an association across estuaries. Uncontrolled ancestry and habitat prevent a confident causal interpretation.
  • The randomized inhibitor experiment supports a causal effect of the assigned inhibitor under the tested greenhouse conditions, provided the comparison remained valid. Its relevance to Tide-1 depends on inhibitor specificity. Among observed outcomes, the difference is approximately 52.1%−74.2%=−22.152.1\% - 74.2\% = -22.1 percentage points.

Step 2: weigh reliability and relevance.

Line Reliability for its measured result Relevance to Tide-1 causing survival Weight in this question
Molecular Good internal contrast: changing the proposed binding sequence removed attachment. Indirect; attachment is upstream of the outcome. Supports a plausible link, not the whole claim.
Organismal edit E1 and E2 separately show lower survival than the pooled sibling controls. Direct for one background and exposure; unintended edits and unreported batch structure remain possible. Important loss-of-function evidence.
Population Large and distributed, but unresolved ancestry and habitat remain. Directly concerns field populations, yet causal relevance is uncertain. Compatible context, not pivotal causal evidence.
Randomized inhibitor Random assignment and masked outcome assessment protect the comparison. Complete-case reporting omits 4% of inhibitor assignments and 3% of vehicle assignments; even the most outcome-adverse allocation of those missing results preserves a lower survival proportion in the inhibitor arm. Direct for the inhibitor's effect; indirect for Tide-1 if the inhibitor is not specific. Important intervention evidence, with uncertainty about exact effect size and a specificity boundary.

“Large” does not automatically mean “heavy.” The population study includes the most plants, but its unresolved confounding makes it less decisive for the causal question than its sample size suggests.

Step 3: inspect convergence and dependence.

The edit and inhibitor experiments use different manipulations, so a single unintended DNA edit would not explain both. The molecular assay uses another method and endpoint. Their biases are not wholly independent, however: all three interpret Tide-1 as the relevant target. If the inhibitor is nonspecific and the edited region alters a neighbouring process, both organismal results could point in the same direction for different reasons.

The population association agrees in direction but carries a separate ancestry-and-habitat threat. It extends the setting, but it cannot rescue a weak experiment merely by agreeing.

Step 4: locate the hinge.

The evidence connects Tide-1 perturbation to survival and Tide-1 to a molecular binding event. It does not yet show that the particular binding event causes the survival difference. That molecule-to-phenotype link is the hinge.

Step 5: state and challenge the result.

The bundle provides moderate support that Tide-1 contributes to survival of Seamarsh grass during the tested 14-day greenhouse salt exposure. The pivotal evidence is the lower survival after two distinct Tide-1-directed perturbations, supported by a sequence-specific molecular interaction. The estuary association is compatible with the claim but is not decisive because ancestry and habitat remain confounded. The evidence does not establish that Tide-1 is the sole salt-tolerance mechanism, that the proposed DNA-binding step mediates the survival effect, or that the effect generalizes to every genetic background or field exposure. Restoring functional Tide-1 in edited plants across several backgrounds, while measuring both target-gene activity and survival, would most directly test the remaining hinge.

The phrase moderate support fits because several different methods converge, yet the target-specificity and cross-scale link remain uncertain. “Tide-1 causes salt tolerance” would be too broad. “Tide-1 is merely associated with survival” would ignore the interventions.

Independent checks.

  • Direction check: both edited lines have lower survival than controls, and the inhibitor arm has lower survival than the vehicle arm.
  • Scale check: the conclusion names greenhouse conditions rather than all estuaries.
  • Rival-explanation check: off-target effects remain possible, so the proposed restoration study must reverse the phenotype through Tide-1 itself.
  • Missing-outcome check: if every missing inhibitor outcome were a survival and every missing vehicle outcome were not, survival would be 54/100=54%54/100 = 54\% versus 72/100=72%72/100 = 72\%. Reversing those assignments gives 50% versus 75%. Thus the inhibitor arm remains lower by 18 to 25 percentage points across these extreme allocations, although the exact effect remains uncertain.
  • Uncertainty check: if the pooled edited and control plants are treated as independent binomial observations, an assumption-dependent 95% interval for the −33-33-percentage-point difference is approximately −45-45 to −21-21 percentage points. That calculation is a sensitivity summary, not a defensible design-based interval, because the supplied data do not describe allocation by edit line, batches, or other dependence.

Self-explanation prompt. Which decision changed the final claim most: the population sample size, the two intervention directions, or the untested molecule-to-phenotype hinge? Explain why the other two do not determine the wording by themselves.

The strongest answer identifies the hinge. Agreement among the interventions raises confidence, while the population line broadens relevance. But the untested link is what prevents a stronger mechanistic claim.

Source Note
claims claim-lines-answer-different-questions, claim-weigh-before-integrate, claim-independent-convergence, claim-randomization-boundary, claim-mechanistic-limits, claim-calibrated-conclusionsources source-efsa-weight-evidence, source-lawlor-triangulation, source-fda-ich-e9, source-iarc-preamble

The sources support the integration procedure, the need to compare differing bias structures, the qualified role of randomization and blinding, the limits of experimental systems, and transparent rationale for a bounded conclusion. Tide-1, Seamarsh grass, all results, calculations, limits, and the restoration study are original fictional teaching material.

scenes.title: Agreement is not a vote count
scenes.contentHtml:
Agreement is not a vote count

Two tempting rules would make evidence integration easy:

  1. four agreeing studies are four votes for the claim;
  2. a randomized intervention settles causation and mechanism.

Both rules fail in predictable ways. Commit to an answer in each case before reading the repair.

Misconception
id misconception-four-voteslabel Four agreeing analyses equal four independent confirmations

Prediction. One cohort is analysed with four statistical methods. All four estimates point in the same direction. A second cohort is unavailable. Has the causal claim been confirmed four independent times?

Write yes or no, then name the feature that determines independence.

Why the model feels reasonable. Repetition often reduces random error, and seeing the same answer several times feels reassuring.

What it cannot explain. All four analyses share the same recruitment, exposure measurement, outcome measurement, missing participants, and unmeasured variables. A shared error can survive every analysis. Changing the calculation is not the same as changing the source of evidence.

More adequate model. Agreement carries more information when the approaches answer the same underlying question but have different key biases. Count neither papers nor methods. Map the dependence.

Compare on the same case. The four analyses may test sensitivity to statistical assumptions, which is useful. They do not by themselves test sensitivity to a mismeasured exposure or an unmeasured confounder shared by the dataset.

Recheck. A molecular assay, a whole-organism gene edit, and a population cohort agree. The assay and edit use one laboratory's antibody, but the cohort does not. Is the bundle fully independent? No. It contains partial convergence: the cohort has a different bias structure, while the two experimental lines share a reagent-dependent vulnerability.

Misconception
id misconception-randomization-proves-alllabel Randomization rules out every rival explanation

Prediction. Seedlings are randomly assigned to inhibitor or vehicle. Investigators know the assignments, a third of inhibitor-assigned seedlings lack outcomes, and the inhibitor can affect a second protein. Does random assignment alone prove that Tide-1 caused the outcome?

Answer before continuing.

Why the model feels reasonable. Random assignment can make comparison groups similar in their starting prognostic factors, including factors the investigators did not measure.

What it cannot explain. Knowledge of assignment can influence care or measurement. Differential missing outcomes can break the original comparison. An off-target intervention can cause the outcome through another route. Randomization concerns assignment; it does not certify every later step or identify the molecular mediator.

More adequate model. A well-conducted randomized comparison supports the causal effect of assignment to the intervention under the studied conditions. To connect that effect to a specific mechanism, add target-specific evidence and preserve the randomized comparison through follow-up and analysis.

Compare on the same case. Concealed assignment and masked outcome assessment protect different parts of the study. Neither makes a three-background greenhouse result automatically apply to every field population.

Recheck. Suppose all assigned seedlings are followed, outcome scoring is masked, and a second target-specific intervention produces the same direction. Confidence in a Tide-1 contribution rises. It still does not establish Tide-1 as the only pathway or the effect in every habitat.

What to do when results disagree

Disagreement is not a cue to hide the inconvenient result or average unlike outcomes. First ask whether the studies really asked the same question.

  • Did they use the same exposure, outcome, dose, timing, and population?
  • Could one method's main bias push upward while another's pushes downward?
  • Are the measurements comparable?
  • Does the apparent conflict locate a failed bridge—for example, a molecular effect that never reaches the organism?

If a difference in scale or timing explains the results, rewrite the claim so each result has a place. If a likely bias explains one result, state that judgment and its evidence. If neither explanation is secure, lower confidence and design a study that makes the competing explanations predict different outcomes.

Check
id check-conflictlabel Retrieval before feedback

A molecular change appears after one hour, but no organismal effect appears after 14 days. Give two explanations that do not require either study to be fraudulent or careless. Then name one measurement that would discriminate between them.

Possible explanations include a transient change that is reversed by feedback, an effect too small to alter the phenotype, an irrelevant tissue, an assay artifact, or compensating pathways in the intact organism. A discriminating measurement must sit between the results: for example, measure the proposed downstream process over time in the relevant tissue while confirming that the molecular intervention reached its target. “Repeat both studies” is not yet discriminating because it does not separate the explanations.

Source Note
claims claim-independent-convergence, claim-randomization-boundary, claim-mechanistic-limits, claim-conflict-response, claim-calibrated-conclusionsources source-efsa-weight-evidence, source-lawlor-triangulation, source-fda-ich-e9, source-iarc-preamble

Lawlor and colleagues support distinguishing triangulation across methods with different biases from repeated analyses that share bias, and using inconsistency to direct further research. FDA/ICH E9 supports the roles of randomization, blinding, protocol conduct, withdrawals, and missing data. EFSA and IARC support explicit uncertainty, system limits, and transparent integration. The cases and repair prompts are original.

scenes.title: Fade the evidence audit
scenes.contentHtml:
Fade the evidence audit

The worked example supplied every decision. These tasks remove support in stages. Attempt all three before reading the feedback section.

1. Complete a calibrated claim

A fictional protein, Glow-2, is being studied in a harmless laboratory alga under low light.

  • A molecular assay shows Glow-2 binds a light-sensing partner.
  • Two independently edited loss-of-function lines grow more slowly than sibling controls under low light but not ordinary light.
  • Across lake samples, the Glow-2 variant is associated with algal abundance, but water temperature was measured only once per lake.
  • A randomized restoration experiment returns growth of edited algae toward the control range; the same laboratory made both edited lines and performed the restoration.

Complete this claim in one paragraph:

The bundle provides ___ support that Glow-2 ___. The pivotal evidence is ___. The lake association ___. Confidence is limited by ___. The claim does not establish ___. A next study should ___ because ___.

Your paragraph must distinguish low-light laboratory growth from abundance in natural lakes.

2. Fill only the missing audit cells

Do not rewrite the whole table. Supply the two missing entries.

Line Reliability or bias Relevance Independence
Molecular binding Clear negative control Upstream of growth Different endpoint, same research programme
Edited lines Two lines, sibling controls Direct for one algal background Shares editing workflow with restoration
Lake association A: ___ Broad setting but indirect for causation Different setting and investigators
Randomized restoration Assignment supports the comparison B: ___ Shares cells and laboratory workflow with edits

Then write one sentence explaining why the randomized restoration is not a completely independent fourth vote.

3. Select stronger convergence

Two research teams address whether fictional Shell-3 contributes to shell thickening in a marine snail.

Bundle P contains four molecular assays. They use different readouts, but all use the same Shell-3 antibody and the same cultured cell line.

Bundle Q contains one molecular assay with a sequence control, a gene-reduction experiment in whole snails, and a population comparison across shorelines. The three results point in the same direction. The whole-snail study uses one laboratory strain, and shoreline temperature may confound the population association.

Choose P, Q, or “cannot compare.” Defend the choice by naming:

  1. one reason for greater convergence;
  2. one remaining shared or line-specific weakness; and
  3. the claim you would allow now.

Feedback after your attempt

1. Completion problem

A defensible answer is:

The bundle provides moderate support that Glow-2 contributes to growth of the tested algal background under low light. The pivotal evidence is the loss of growth in two edited lines and its return after restoration, with the binding assay providing a plausible molecular link. The lake association is compatible but not decisive because the temperature measurement may not represent the relevant exposure. Confidence is limited by shared editing and laboratory dependencies and by uncertain field confounding. The claim does not establish that Glow-2 determines abundance across natural lakes. A next study should repeat target-specific restoration in additional backgrounds with time-resolved light and temperature measurements because that would test both generality and the field alternative.

Other strength words can be defensible if the reasons change with them. An answer that says only “four studies prove Glow-2 causes abundance” ignores scope, dependence, and the difference between growth and abundance.

If your paragraph omitted the population boundary, return to relevance: ask whether the study measured the same outcome under the same conditions. If it omitted shared laboratory dependence, return to independence.

2. Missing cells

  • A: A single temperature measurement can misclassify the relevant temperature exposure and leave residual confounding.
  • B: Direct for restoration of growth in the edited background, but generality to other backgrounds and natural lakes remains open.

The restoration and edit are valuable together because reversal strengthens the target story. They are not wholly independent because they share biological material, editing decisions, and laboratory procedures. If you treated them as independent, draw a line between every pair of studies that shares samples, reagents, personnel, instruments, or untested assumptions.

3. Stronger convergence

Bundle Q offers stronger convergence for a bounded Shell-3 claim because its molecular, organismal, and population approaches have more distinct key biases than Bundle P's antibody- and cell-line-dependent assays. Q is still limited by one organismal strain and possible shoreline confounding. A suitable claim is: “The evidence moderately supports Shell-3 as a contributor to shell thickening in the tested laboratory strain; the shoreline pattern is compatible but not causally resolved.”

“Cannot compare” is defensible only if you explain that essential quality information is missing and therefore refuse to infer from method labels alone. Choosing Q because it has “more levels” without discussing quality and bias earns no credit.

Retry route

Classify your main error before retrying:

  • Contribution error: you asked one line to answer a question it cannot see. Restate its narrow result.
  • Weighting error: you treated sample size or randomization as an automatic trump card. Re-audit reliability and relevance.
  • Dependence error: you counted shared samples, reagents, or assumptions as independent confirmation. Draw the dependence map.
  • Calibration error: your conclusion omitted strength, scope, uncertainty, or the next discriminating test. Rebuild the five-part claim.

Retry the missed task with Shell-3 replacing Glow-2, or Glow-2 replacing Shell-3. Preserve the reasoning structure but do not reuse the completed sentence.

Source Note
claims claim-lines-answer-different-questions, claim-weigh-before-integrate, claim-independent-convergence, claim-randomization-boundary, claim-mechanistic-limits, claim-conflict-response, claim-calibrated-conclusionsources source-efsa-weight-evidence, source-lawlor-triangulation, source-fda-ich-e9, source-iarc-preamble

The sources support the distinctions used in the feedback: evidence must be reliable and relevant before integration, agreement is more informative across differing bias structures, interventions retain implementation and scope limits, experimental systems require relevance checks, and conclusions should expose uncertainty. Glow-2, Shell-3, all evidence bundles, feedback, and retry variants are original fictional material.

scenes.title: When the field result disagrees
scenes.contentHtml:
When the field result disagrees

The worked example moved from a molecular interaction to a greenhouse survival outcome. This transfer changes the causal structure: the intervention changes an environmental cue, while the proposed mechanism concerns a receptor. You must decide which link a null result actually tests.

All organisms, sites, substances, measurements, and results below are fictional. This is a document-based reasoning task, not an instruction to conduct field or laboratory work.

The Harbor-7 bundle

Researchers ask whether a dissolved cue, C, triggers shell thickening in a marine snail through receptor Harbor-7.

  1. Molecular line: purified C binds Harbor-7, but not a Harbor-7 variant with a changed binding pocket.
  2. Organismal line: under a constant aquarium concentration of C, snails with reduced Harbor-7 expression show little shell thickening, while control snails thicken their shells. Growth rate is similar between groups.
  3. Population line: shore sites with higher average C have snails with thicker shells. Wave exposure and predator abundance also differ among sites.
  4. Field intervention: cages at six shore sites are randomly assigned to receive C or vehicle at dawn. Shell thickness after four weeks does not differ detectably. A validation sample shows that sunlight and water exchange reduce C to below the assay's detection limit within one hour on most dosing days.

Your task

Without looking back at the Tide-1 claim, produce four outputs.

  1. Narrow results: write one sentence for what each line supports before integration.
  2. Conflict diagnosis: state whether the field result contradicts Harbor-7 binding, the constant-exposure organismal result, field relevance, or some combination. Explain.
  3. Calibrated claim: name strength, scope, pivotal evidence, uncertainty, and what the evidence does not establish.
  4. Discriminating study: propose one ethical, supervised study design in supplied-data form. State the two rival explanations and the different result each predicts. Do not propose collecting live specimens or releasing substances yourself.

Pause here until you have written all four.

Feedback and one defensible solution

The molecular line supports binding under the assay conditions. The organismal line supports a Harbor-7-dependent response to a maintained concentration of C in the tested aquarium system. The population line supports a field association but cannot separate C from waves and predators. The field intervention supports no detectable effect of the brief dawn assignment as implemented; it does not show that the snails experienced the sustained C exposure used in the aquarium.

The null field result therefore weakens a claim that one dawn dose changes shell thickness under ordinary field loss processes. It does not directly refute receptor binding or a response to maintained C. The exposure-validation result explains why the two interventions may have tested different treatment conditions.

A calibrated claim is:

The evidence provides moderate support that Harbor-7 participates in shell thickening when the tested snails receive a maintained aquarium concentration of C. Molecular binding and the Harbor-7 reduction study are pivotal. The shore association is compatible but confounded, and the randomized field assignment produced no detectable effect under rapidly declining exposure. The bundle does not establish that natural variation in C causes field shell differences or that a brief field pulse engages the proposed pathway. A supervised field study that verifies and maintains a prespecified C exposure, while masking outcome measurement, would distinguish “the pathway does not operate in the field” from “the original field intervention did not deliver the relevant exposure.”

For the discriminating study, the rival predictions must separate:

  • delivery failure: maintained, verified exposure should restore the shell response;
  • field-context failure: even maintained, verified exposure should not produce the aquarium response.

That design still requires ecological safety, permissions, and specialist supervision. For this lesson, analysing a supplied synthetic dataset is the complete equivalent route.

Boundary case

Change one fact: suppose C remained at the prespecified concentration throughout the field study, the assay could detect the relevant biological response, and follow-up was complete. The null result would then weigh more strongly against generalizing the aquarium effect to those field conditions. It still would not undo the molecular binding observation; it would narrow the biological consequence of that binding.

If your first claim said “the field trial proves Harbor-7 is irrelevant,” revise the scope. If it ignored the field result, revise the relevance audit. If your next study simply repeats the original dawn dose, revise the design so the rival explanations make different predictions.

Source Note
claims claim-lines-answer-different-questions, claim-weigh-before-integrate, claim-independent-convergence, claim-randomization-boundary, claim-mechanistic-limits, claim-conflict-response, claim-calibrated-conclusionsources source-efsa-weight-evidence, source-lawlor-triangulation, source-fda-ich-e9, source-iarc-preamble

The sources support preserving exposure, timing, system, and bias boundaries when comparing evidence; treating randomization as one design protection; using inconsistencies to locate further research; and integrating streams through an explicit rationale. Harbor-7, cue C, the evidence bundle, the alternative predictions, and the supplied-data accessibility route are original fictional material.

scenes.title: Rebuild the claim without the lesson
scenes.contentHtml:
Rebuild the claim without the lesson

Close the earlier scenes. On a blank page, reconstruct the path from a research question to a calibrated claim. Use no more than seven lines.

Then answer two cumulative questions:

  1. Where does the association–causation distinction enter the path?
  2. Where do model assumptions and limits enter the path?

Do this before reading the reconstruction below.

A compact reconstruction

  1. State one bounded causal question with exposure, outcome, system or population, and timing.
  2. Translate every study into the narrow result it actually supports.
  3. Weigh reliability: design, measurement, missingness, and bias control.
  4. Weigh relevance: match exposure, outcome, system, dose, time, and target population.
  5. Compare consistency and map shared samples, tools, investigators, and assumptions.
  6. Explain conflict or mark it as unresolved; identify the weakest causal or mechanistic bridge.
  7. Write direction, strength, scope, pivotal support, uncertainty, exclusion, and next discriminating test.

Association versus causation enters when you translate each line and inspect its design. Model limits enter when you judge relevance across molecular systems, organisms, populations, and interventions. They return in the final scope statement.

If your reconstruction said only “collect four types and see whether they agree,” add weighting and dependence. If it listed every possible bias but never reached a claim, add the final integration step: uncertainty should calibrate a conclusion, not prevent all conclusions.

Mastery check

The assessment contains two equivalent, untimed forms. Complete Form A first:

  • Evidence contribution and boundary
  • Quality and independence of convergence
  • An integrated, calibrated claim
  • Conflict diagnosis and a discriminating study

Each form is worth 16 points. Mastery-quality evidence requires at least 13 points and at least one point on every rubric criterion; a high total cannot compensate for omitting a whole dimension such as scope or conflict diagnosis. Attempt each item before opening its answer, reasoning, or common-error feedback.

If you do not yet meet the criterion, classify the error, use the route below, and complete Form B rather than replaying the same answers:

  • Evidence contribution and boundary — new case
  • Quality and independence of convergence — new case
  • An integrated, calibrated claim — new case
  • Conflict diagnosis and a discriminating study — new case

After both forms, a further retry should use a new fictional bundle with the same four demands: distinguish contributions, weigh convergence, calibrate a claim, and resolve a conflict. Changing only names or option order is not an adequate retry.

Recovery route

  • You confuse evidence labels with rank. For each line, write “can see” and “cannot see.” Remember that an intervention can also be organismal or population-level.
  • You treat association as causation. Draw one plausible third variable that affects both exposure and outcome. Then ask what design feature blocks that path.
  • You treat a model as the target system. Complete: “The model represents ___ under ___; it omits ___; carrying the result to ___ requires ___.”
  • You count studies. Draw links between studies that share data, reagents, personnel, instruments, or assumptions. Reassess the remaining independent convergence.
  • You let randomization prove too much. Name the assigned intervention, then audit concealment, blinding, adherence, attrition, measurement, target specificity, and scope.
  • You avoid a conclusion because one line conflicts. Check question, timing, exposure delivery, and expected bias direction. Narrow the claim or name the unresolved hinge and a discriminating test.
  • Your claim sounds certain but says little. Use the seven-part reconstruction and require a strength word with reasons, not by itself.

Return to Form B only after you can explain the repaired step aloud in ordinary language.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every learning operation in this lesson has a text route. The diagram is reproduced as an ordered relationship in prose and as a table. No distinction depends on colour, position, animation, sound, hover, dragging, speed, or pointer use. Prompts, evidence bundles, feedback, citations, and assessment logic remain in semantic source order for keyboard, screen-reader, narrow-screen, 200% zoom/reflow, print, and reduced-motion use.

You may answer in prose, headings, bullets, or a table, and you may type or dictate using your own assistive technology. A diagrammed dependence map can be replaced by a list of study pairs and their shared feature. No physical field or laboratory action, image upload, audio, personal record, or personal health information is required. The supplied fictional datasets preserve the same evidence-integration operation as any visual or practical route.

Schedule retrieval beyond today

  • After about 48 hours, reconstruct the seven steps and write one sentence explaining why agreement across different biases matters.
  • After about one week, take a scientific claim from your current reading and identify its strongest evidence line, weakest bridge, and unjustified word, if any.
  • When you next study how claims depend on evidence and assumptions, retrieve the calibrated-claim structure before reading the new material. Use it to test whether an unstated assumption changes the claim's scope.

These delayed checks are learning opportunities, not proof of mastery by completion. The assessment and later transfer performance provide the evidence.

Source Note
claims claim-lines-answer-different-questions, claim-weigh-before-integrate, claim-independent-convergence, claim-randomization-boundary, claim-mechanistic-limits, claim-conflict-response, claim-calibrated-conclusionsources source-efsa-weight-evidence, source-lawlor-triangulation, source-fda-ich-e9, source-iarc-preamble

The sources support the reconstructed integration process, the importance of different bias structures, the qualified interpretation of randomization, cross-system limits, conflict-directed research, and transparent statements of evidence strength and uncertainty. The mastery rule, alternate forms, recovery routes, accessibility equivalents, and retrieval schedule are original lesson design.

aiAssistance.disclosure: VSBDev (github:VSBDev) is accountable. Four isolated Anthropic Claude CLI runs using claude-fable-5 supplied blind science-only fragments; those runs did not receive repository, Premed, outcome-ID, or packaging context. Separate actor records preserve every accepted session and prompt digest. OpenAI Codex, model gpt-5.6-sol, provided material editorial, research-verification, pedagogical, assessment, accessibility, rights-ledger, and packaging assistance in run f3c86893-e6c3-4283-9387-73f1584e6e82. Codex was not the blind science author. Full boundaries and provenance are in ATTRIBUTION.md.

assessment.title: Mechanistic and convergent evidence mastery check

items.prompt: A purified-protein assay shows that fictional receptor Reed-2 binds signal S, while a changed binding pocket does not. No cells or organisms were tested. Which conclusion best matches this evidence?

options.text: Reed-2 can bind S under the assay conditions; consequences in cells, organisms, and populations remain open.
Reed-2 causes the organismal response to S.
S will produce the same response in every population carrying Reed-2.
Molecular evidence cannot contribute to a causal explanation.

answer.summary: The assay directly supports a bounded molecular interaction, not its downstream consequences.
answer.reasoning: The changed-pocket contrast strengthens the binding interpretation within the assay. The experiment does not include cellular regulation, an intact organism, or a population, so carrying the result to those outcomes requires additional evidence and model assumptions.
commonErrors: Treating a molecular link as the complete organismal mechanism.
Rejecting molecular evidence entirely because it cannot establish the whole causal chain.
Generalizing across populations that were never studied.

items.prompt: Select the two statements that correctly describe convergent evidence.
items.stimulus: A programme has one cohort analysed four ways, one whole-organism intervention, and two molecular assays that use the same antibody.

options.text: Agreement is more informative when approaches address the same question but have different key sources of bias.
The four analyses of one cohort count as four independent confirmations.
The two molecular assays may share a vulnerability because both depend on the same antibody.
Whichever study has the largest sample should determine the conclusion regardless of design or relevance.

answer.summary: Convergence depends on question alignment and differing bias structures, while shared tools can create shared error.
answer.reasoning: Repeated analyses of one cohort can test statistical assumptions but retain the cohort's recruitment, measurement, missingness, and unmeasured-confounding vulnerabilities. The two molecular assays differ in readout but can repeat an antibody artifact. The organismal intervention may add a more distinct bias structure, subject to its own quality and relevance.
commonErrors: Counting analytical methods instead of mapping shared data and biases.
Assuming different readouts make shared-reagent assays fully independent.
Using sample size as a substitute for reliability and relevance.

items.prompt: Write one calibrated claim about Leaf-8. Include strength, scope, pivotal support, uncertainty, one exclusion, and a next discriminating test.
items.stimulus: Fictional evidence: Leaf-8 binds a water-channel regulator in a molecular assay. In one plant background, two Leaf-8 loss-of-function lines close their pores less after a controlled dry-air challenge than sibling controls. Across farms, a Leaf-8 variant is associated with crop survival, but irrigation practice and ancestry covary with the variant. A randomly assigned, target-specific restoration returns pore closure toward the control range in one edited line. All data are fictional.

answer.correct: The bundle provides moderate support that Leaf-8 contributes to pore closure after the tested dry-air challenge in the studied plant background. The two loss-of-function results and target-specific restoration are pivotal, while molecular binding supplies a plausible link. The farm association is compatible but is not decisive because irrigation and ancestry remain confounded. The evidence does not establish that Leaf-8 is the sole pathway or that it determines survival across farms. Repeating restoration in additional backgrounds while measuring regulator activity and pore closure would test generality and the proposed bridge.
answer.summary: A strong response integrates the interventions and molecular link while narrowing the population and mechanistic claims.
answer.reasoning: Loss of function followed by target-specific restoration gives the most direct support for a Leaf-8 contribution under the controlled challenge. The molecular assay does not by itself connect binding to the phenotype, and the population association cannot isolate Leaf-8 from farm practices and ancestry. The final claim must preserve both contributions and limits.
acceptableResponses: Any response that uses defensible strength language with reasons, restricts the causal statement to the tested challenge and background, identifies loss-of-function plus restoration as pivotal, treats the farm evidence as confounded, states a meaningful exclusion, and proposes a study that tests either cross-background generality or the binding-to-phenotype bridge.
commonErrors: Writing that Leaf-8 causes farm survival without resolving confounding.
Calling the evidence weak solely because no single study answers every scale.
Omitting the tested background or dry-air challenge from scope.
Proposing only to repeat the same assay instead of testing the open bridge.

items.prompt: Explain what the null field result does and does not contradict, then propose one discriminating study with different predictions for two rival explanations.
items.stimulus: In fictional laboratory chambers, Frost-6 activity rises during a maintained six-hour cooling period, and Frost-6-reduced seeds survive that period less often. In a randomized field assignment, a cooling treatment has no detectable effect on survival. Sensors show that the assigned field cooling lasted only eight minutes. All data are fictional; analyse the supplied summaries only.

answer.correct: The field result supports no detectable effect of an eight-minute cooling assignment; it does not directly test the maintained six-hour exposure or refute the laboratory Frost-6 response. A supervised study that successfully assigns and verifies six hours of cooling in the relevant field setting would discriminate delivery-duration failure from field-context failure: an effect after verified six-hour cooling supports the first explanation, whereas a null result despite verified exposure supports the second.
answer.summary: The apparent conflict partly reflects different delivered exposures, so the next study must separate duration from field context.
answer.reasoning: A null effect under an eight-minute exposure and an effect under a six-hour exposure need not answer the same causal question. Verifying the intended duration in the target context makes the rival explanations predict different results and therefore creates a discriminating test.
acceptableResponses: Any response that distinguishes the assigned field intervention from the delivered exposure, preserves the bounded laboratory result, and proposes an ethical supplied-data or supervised study in which verified duration makes delivery failure and field-context failure predict different outcomes.
commonErrors: Declaring Frost-6 irrelevant because the field assignment was null.
Ignoring the field result and claiming universal field relevance.
Repeating the same eight-minute field assignment without testing exposure duration.

items.prompt: In a controlled whole-organism study, investigators randomly assign otherwise comparable fictional insects from one laboratory strain to a target-specific procedure that reduces Wing-4 expression or to a matched control procedure. The reduced-expression group recovers more slowly from a defined heat exposure. Follow-up is complete, and no natural populations were studied. Which conclusion is best calibrated?

options.text: Wing-4 contributes to recovery after the tested heat exposure in the studied strain; mechanism and population generality remain open.
Wing-4 determines heat survival in every insect population.
The complete molecular mechanism of heat recovery has been established.
Because the study is organismal, it can support only an uncontrolled association.

answer.summary: The organismal intervention supports a bounded contribution in one strain, not a complete mechanism or universal effect.
answer.reasoning: The randomly assigned, target-specific reduction and matched control procedure support a bounded causal contribution to the studied organismal response. The design does not reveal every molecular link and does not include the genetic and environmental variation of natural populations.
commonErrors: Treating organismal evidence as inherently observational.
Extending a one-strain result to every population.
Confusing a phenotype effect with a complete molecular mechanism.

items.prompt: Select the two statements that correctly weigh this evidence bundle.
items.stimulus: Three laboratories report the same molecular effect, but all received aliquots from one cell preparation. A fourth study uses a blinded whole-organism intervention and reports the same direction in one strain.

options.text: The three molecular reports share a biological-sample dependency despite coming from different laboratories.
The organismal intervention adds a more distinct method and endpoint, subject to its one-strain scope.
All four studies deserve equal weight because all point in the same direction.
Blinding makes the organismal result generalize automatically to every strain and environment.

answer.summary: Different laboratories do not remove dependence on one cell preparation, while the organismal intervention adds distinct evidence with bounded scope.
answer.reasoning: The molecular studies can repeat a sample-specific feature or artifact. Blinding protects outcome assessment in the organismal study but does not establish external validity. The combined evidence is more diverse than four equal votes and must be weighted accordingly.
commonErrors: Using laboratory identity as the only test of independence.
Treating blinding as a guarantee of generalizability.
Assigning equal weight without comparing quality, relevance, and dependence.

items.prompt: Write one calibrated claim about Wave-9. Include strength, scope, pivotal support, uncertainty, one exclusion, and a next discriminating test.
items.stimulus: Fictional evidence: Wave-9 changes conformation when salinity falls in a purified assay. Fish with reduced Wave-9 expression recover ion balance more slowly after a controlled salinity change in one strain. Across river populations, a Wave-9 variant is associated with migration success, but water temperature and ancestry differ. A randomized target-specific restoration improves ion-balance recovery in the reduced-expression fish. All data are fictional.

answer.correct: The bundle provides moderate support that Wave-9 contributes to ion-balance recovery after the tested salinity change in the studied fish strain. Reduced expression and target-specific restoration are pivotal, while the molecular change is compatible with a sensing role. The river association is not decisive because temperature and ancestry remain confounded. The evidence does not establish that Wave-9 determines migration success or acts identically across populations. Repeating target-specific restoration across strains while measuring the proposed downstream ion-transport process would test generality and mechanism.
answer.summary: A defensible claim centres the controlled phenotype and restoration while keeping the field association and molecular link in their proper scope.
answer.reasoning: The organismal intervention and restoration most directly support a Wave-9 contribution under the tested conditions. The purified assay supplies a plausible upstream link, and the population line broadens context without resolving causation. The next test should address an actual gap rather than repeat the association.
acceptableResponses: Any response that gives justified strength, limits the causal statement to the tested strain and salinity change, identifies reduction plus restoration as pivotal, treats migration success as unresolved, and proposes a target-specific cross-strain or downstream-mechanism test.
commonErrors: Claiming Wave-9 causes migration success from the population association.
Ignoring the purified system's distance from an organismal outcome.
Omitting strength or uncertainty.
Repeating only the existing river comparison.

items.prompt: Explain whether the two intervention results test the same claim, revise the conclusion, and propose one discriminating measurement or study.
items.stimulus: Fictional evidence: a target-specific intervention changes Root-3 activity and nutrient uptake when applied before root development. A second randomized intervention applied after roots are mature changes Root-3 activity but not uptake. Assignment, follow-up, and measurement are sound in both studies. All data are fictional.

answer.correct: The interventions test different developmental timing, so the results support a timing-bounded claim: changing Root-3 before root development affects later nutrient uptake, whereas changing it after maturation does not under the studied conditions. Measuring the proposed developmental intermediate repeatedly from early development through maturity, with the intervention assigned at several prespecified times, would distinguish an early critical window from a mechanism that should operate continuously.
answer.summary: The apparent conflict reveals effect timing rather than automatically invalidating either well-conducted study.
answer.reasoning: Both interventions change Root-3 activity, but only one occurs before the organismal structure develops. A developmental-window explanation predicts a graded loss of uptake effect after the critical period, whereas a continuously operating mechanism predicts an effect even after maturity.
acceptableResponses: Any response that preserves both well-conducted results, restricts the causal claim by developmental timing, and proposes measurements or interventions whose timing separates a critical-window explanation from continuous action.
commonErrors: Averaging the two results as if timing were irrelevant.
Discarding one study despite sound design and a material difference in the question.
Proposing an exact repeat without measuring the timing-dependent bridge.

criteria.description: Identifies the narrow contribution that the evidence line can make to the specified question.
Preserves a material scale, design, population, timing, or mechanism boundary.
Checks that evidence addresses the same underlying question and considers reliability and relevance before agreement.
Identifies an important shared or differing source of bias rather than counting studies as equal votes.
Uses the pivotal evidence lines to justify the relation and its strength rather than listing studies without synthesis.
States scope, uncertainty, an exclusion, and a next test that addresses a material open bridge.
Explains whether apparently conflicting results address the same exposure, timing, system, outcome, and causal link.
Proposes a bounded study or measurement for which plausible rival explanations predict different results.

sources.title: Guidance on the use of the weight of evidence approach in scientific assessments
sources.license: CC BY-ND 4.0, as displayed in the PMC record. No source expression is adapted in this pack.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson independently synthesizes the underlying framework and does not copy or adapt source prose, organization, examples, tables, figures, questions, or other expression.

agentAccess.notes: NCBI usage policies and the public robots route were checked. Because general crawling of PMC article pages is disallowed, substantive verification used one public NCBI BioC API request, an allowed article API route, without login, paywall, bulk retrieval, or access-control bypass.

sources.title: Triangulation in aetiological epidemiology
sources.publisher: International Journal of Epidemiology, Oxford University Press
sources.locator: Abstract; Table 1; key messages; concluding remarks; DOI 10.1093/ije/dyw314
sources.license: CC BY 4.0, as displayed in the PMC record.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson uses the methodological facts as evidence but independently creates its wording, organization, examples, dependence maps, prompts, and assessment items.

agentAccess.notes: NCBI usage policies and the public robots route were checked. Because general crawling of PMC article pages is disallowed, substantive verification used one public NCBI BioC API request, an allowed article API route, without login, paywall, bulk retrieval, or access-control bypass.

sources.title: E9 Statistical Principles for Clinical Trials
creators: International Council for Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Pharmaceuticals for Human Use
sources.locator: Sections 2.3.1-2.3.2 on blinding and randomization; PDF pages 12-14
sources.license: FDA website content is public domain unless otherwise noted under the FDA Website Policies; underlying ICH interests are respected. Use here is facts-only with no copied expression.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson independently explains the functions and limits of randomization, blinding, protocol conduct, withdrawals, and missing outcomes without reproducing the guidance's wording, structure, examples, or tables.

sources.title: Preamble to the IARC Monographs
creators: International Agency for Research on Cancer
sources.publisher: International Agency for Research on Cancer, World Health Organization
sources.locator: Part A section 6; Part B sections 2-6, especially PDF pages 10-12, 18, 24-39
sources.license: Copyright IARC/WHO 2019; IARC Publications Terms allow limited educational review. No source expression, table, or figure is reused or adapted.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The source is used to verify the existence, separate evaluation, integration, and stated limits of human, experimental-animal, and mechanistic evidence streams. All lesson language, examples, diagram, and assessments are independently created.

agentAccess.notes: The IARC Publications Terms and the Monographs robots route were checked before substantive use. No term prohibited this limited agent-assisted educational factual review; the public PDF required no login, paywall, bulk retrieval, or access-control bypass.

claims.statement: Molecular or mechanistic, organismal, population, and intervention evidence can contribute different information to one causal question, and the relevance of each line depends on the system, population, exposure, outcome, and timing being claimed.
claims.scope: General evidence integration for bounded biological and causal questions at college-introductory level.
claims.uncertainty: The four labels are teaching categories rather than mutually exclusive or universally exhaustive classes; intervention describes design while the other labels often describe scale.

claims.statement: A transparent weight-of-evidence assessment assembles evidence into lines, weighs their reliability, relevance, and consistency, integrates them, and retains uncertainty about both the evidence and the weighting process.
claims.scope: Qualitative or quantitative scientific assessments that integrate several pieces or lines of evidence.
claims.uncertainty: Reliability, relevance, and consistency are a general framework; specialized domains may require additional criteria and formal methods.

claims.statement: Agreement strengthens causal inference most when approaches address the same underlying question but have different, unrelated key sources of bias; repeated analyses that share data and biases are not independent confirmations.
claims.scope: Triangulation for causal questions, especially when expected bias structures can be compared across methods or settings.
claims.uncertainty: Key biases and their directions may be incompletely known; apparent methodological diversity does not guarantee independence.

claims.statement: Random assignment tends to balance known and unknown prognostic factors and supports treatment-effect comparisons, but concealment, blinding, protocol deviations, adherence, withdrawals, missing outcomes, target specificity, and study scope still affect the inference.
claims.scope: Randomized intervention comparisons in clinical or analogous experimental settings, with conclusions bounded to the assigned intervention and studied conditions.
claims.uncertainty: The exact impact of each implementation failure depends on the design, data-generating process, analysis, and target estimand.

claims.statement: Mechanistic findings can identify plausible pathway links, but test-system capability, species or tissue relevance, exposure conditions, and coherence across systems determine how strongly they support a broader organismal or population claim.
claims.scope: Use of mechanistic evidence and experimental systems to support or limit inference across biological scales.
claims.uncertainty: The source framework is designed for carcinogenic-hazard evaluation; this lesson transfers only its general evidence-stream and model-relevance principles to fictional biological cases.

claims.statement: When evidence lines conflict, investigators should compare question, timing, measurement, assumptions, and expected bias direction, then lower confidence or seek a study that discriminates among explanations rather than hide or blindly average the discrepancy.
claims.scope: Qualitative integration of divergent results that are intended to inform the same bounded causal question.
claims.uncertainty: A discrepancy may reflect real effect modification, different questions, chance, bias, or model failure; evidence integration alone may not identify which explanation is correct.

claims.statement: An integrated conclusion should state the relative strength and scope of support, identify pivotal evidence and remaining uncertainty, explain the rationale, and name evidence that could materially change the conclusion.
claims.scope: Transparent qualitative evidence conclusions; the lesson's strength words are not a universal quantitative scale.
claims.uncertainty: Different formal assessment systems use different descriptive categories, thresholds, and decision rules.

terms.definition: One coherent group of observations or studies that contributes a particular kind of support to a specified scientific question.
Evidence about molecular identity, structure, interaction, reaction, or change that can test one or more links in a proposed mechanism.
Evidence about a response or phenotype in an intact organism, where many tissues, processes, behaviours, and environmental inputs can interact.
Evidence about patterns, variation, associations, or effects across a defined group of people or other organisms, settings, or times.
Evidence from a design in which an investigator deliberately assigns or changes an exposure, treatment, process, or condition and compares outcomes.
Evidence that bears on the entities, interactions, sequence, and constraints proposed to produce an outcome.
The extent to which the information in a piece or line of evidence is credible for what it reports, given its design, measurement, conduct, analysis, and reporting.

alignment.note: Lesson 6 defines reliability as the consistency of a measurement across repeated readings. Here reliability is the broader evidence-appraisal sense: the credibility of a whole line of evidence. A measurement can be reliable in the Lesson 6 sense yet sit inside evidence judged not credible here.

terms.definition: The contribution reliable evidence would make to the specified question, considering the match in exposure, outcome, system, population, dose, and timing.
The extent to which contributions from different pieces or lines of evidence are compatible for the specified question after differences in design and scope are considered.
A pattern in which the main potential errors of one approach differ from and are not caused by the main potential errors of another approach.
A conclusion whose causal language, strength, scope, and certainty are limited to what the integrated evidence warrants.
The evidence line or result that carries the greatest justified influence on the integrated conclusion for the specified question.
Distortion of an exposure-outcome comparison by another factor related to both the exposure and the outcome, such that the observed association does not isolate the exposure's effect.
Assignment by a chance process that gives each eligible unit a defined allocation possibility and tends to balance prognostic factors between comparison groups.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson identity and licence

  • Lesson: PREM-SCI-011 — From evidence lines to a calibrated claim
  • Accountable principal: VSBDev (github:VSBDev)
  • Course-content licence: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)
  • Author-stage state: draft, source confidence pending-review

VSBDev is accountable for the scientific, editorial, pedagogical, accessibility, rights, and provenance record in this pack. The local authenticated GitHub identity check returned VSBDev on 2026-07-21.

Blind science authorship

A blind science-authoring stage supplied four accepted baseline fragments. Each fragment came from a separate isolated Claude CLI call that received only its science prompt. The blind calls did not receive this repository, EmbeddedKnowledge, Premed, the graph outcome, the lesson ID, the pack format, or the editorial-packaging prompt. The accepted dossier reported claude-fable-5 in machine-readable modelUsage for every retained call; it also reported claude-haiku-4-5-20251001. All Opus-routed outputs were excluded before this packaging run. The Claude CLI version was not present in the accepted dossier and is recorded as not supplied rather than inferred.

The four accepted science-author records are preserved separately because four isolated sessions cannot honestly be collapsed into one agent identity:

Fragment Scientific contribution to the baseline Claude session / run ID Prompt SHA-256 Disclosed model record
1 Questions molecular experiments can address, including interaction, structure, composition, kinetics, and energetic or environmental response 6257e56c-c2a9-4dbd-b390-955656f27cf7 sha256:95471aa2ca876f49fa864397e45cd34eaf165a99a440a6b4571362a6058b6859 Claude CLI / Anthropic / claude-fable-5; CLI version not supplied; modelUsage also listed claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
2 Intervention, randomization, controls, causal comparison, and effect direction or size 94c8e426-2cbb-438b-85d6-436370aa1329 sha256:3bffe185040c30107eed5cd607236a14ac600e1ed59c0399b10cc80b3bf1e367 Claude CLI / Anthropic / claude-fable-5; CLI version not supplied; modelUsage also listed claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
3 Deliberate manipulation and controlled comparison as a route toward causal inference 969c4dcc-f292-4b99-91e4-f86a91ce242e sha256:b921030e0f996d184d633efbfe0412fabe61948342bdeecd4354b8b08d8e7a43 Claude CLI / Anthropic / claude-fable-5; CLI version not supplied; modelUsage also listed claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
4 Quality- and independence-sensitive evidence weighting, transparent treatment of disagreement, provisional conclusions, and discriminating research 33086b45-c274-4f62-b3b9-e02d10afc9db sha256:34796267d2772f05abdccc7bb3fbc9c57874eefe2739f758c42bd8f8aa5fc2cf Claude CLI / Anthropic / claude-fable-5; CLI version not supplied; modelUsage also listed claude-haiku-4-5-20251001

These runs are material blind science authorship, not citations or independent academic verification. Their fragments served as a scientific first draft and were checked against the sources in references.json before incorporation.

Editorial and pedagogical packaging assistance

Material editorial, research-verification, pedagogical, assessment, accessibility, rights-ledger, and Lesson Format v1 packaging assistance used Codex by OpenAI, model gpt-5.6-sol, version Codex CLI 0.144.6, in unique run f3c86893-e6c3-4283-9387-73f1584e6e82.

The packager material-instructions digest is sha256:b455458a8b61d4ba298d736bcac65aa87414822c39062b8b627c10c6bf434ffd, supplied through EK_PACKAGER_PROMPT_SHA256. It identifies the exact discloseable packaging prompt supplied for this run. It does not claim to cover hidden provider, system, runtime, or tool instructions that cannot be exported.

Codex is not represented as the blind science author. It received the repository context, assigned outcome, accepted dossier, packaging requirements, and provenance instructions needed to verify, correct, teach, structure, and validate the material.

Scientific corrections to the baseline

The intervention fragments were directionally useful but too categorical. The lesson corrects them in four material ways:

  • random assignment tends to balance prognostic factors; it does not literally guarantee balance or remove every source of bias;
  • manipulation supports a bounded effect of the assigned intervention only when assignment, follow-up, measurement, adherence, analysis, and other design conditions preserve the comparison;
  • an intervention's effect does not by itself identify the molecular mechanism or eliminate off-target explanations;
  • reliable conclusions are scoped to the studied units, intervention, outcome, timing, and conditions rather than presented as automatically reproducible or universal.

The conflict fragment's core advice is retained and made operational: compare question, reliability, relevance, expected bias, and dependence; disclose unresolved discrepancies; and choose a study for which competing explanations predict different results.

Original synthesis and source use

The opening, four-window explanation, evidence table, causal-bridge procedure, calibrated-claim structure, Tide-1 worked example, misconception repairs, Glow-2, Shell-3, Harbor-7, Leaf-8, Frost-6, Wing-4, Wave-9, and Root-3 cases, all numerical values, assessment forms, feedback, recovery routes, and declarative diagram are original course material created for this pack. All named organisms, molecules, interventions, sites, studies, and data are explicitly fictional.

The four works in references.json were used only as factual and methodological authorities. No source wording, organization, example, question, table, figure, media, or dataset was copied, translated, adapted, or redistributed. The EFSA work's CC BY-ND status and IARC's restricted publication terms therefore do not supply or need to supply a reuse basis for lesson expression; every use is facts-only original expression.
